title: How to rebuild a sense of home in your own body
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

htmlContent:
The body doesn't forget where it came from

I spent years trying to leave my body. I thought if I could just think my way out of the pain, or work my way out of the shame, or meditate my way out of the terror, I would finally feel safe. I read the books, I went to the retreats, I bought the crystals. And none of it worked. Because the problem wasn't that I hadn't tried hard enough. The problem was that my nervous system had learned, over years and years, that the body was not a safe place to live.

Here's the thing. When you grow up in an environment that is unpredictable, or hostile, or neglectful, your body becomes a war zone. You learn to brace. You learn to disconnect. You learn that the only way to survive is to get out of your own skin. And that works, for a while. It gets you through. But eventually, you realize you've been living in a kind of exile, floating somewhere above yourself, watching your life happen to someone else.

I remember the first time I felt my feet on the ground and didn't immediately want to run. I was standing in my kitchen, making tea, and suddenly I noticed that my shoulders weren't up by my ears. My jaw wasn't clenched. My breath was moving, slowly, in and out. And I started to cry. Not because I was sad, but because I had just realized that my body had never, not once in my entire life, felt like a place I could rest.

That moment changed something. It showed me that rebuilding a sense of home in your own body is possible. But it's not about fixing yourself. It's about returning. It's about coming back to a place you were forced to abandon. And that takes time, and patience, and a kind of gentleness that most of us have never been shown.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body, not in the story. You can talk about what happened to you for years, and your nervous system will still be locked in the same patterns of fight, flight, or freeze. The body doesn't forget where it came from. But it can learn, slowly, that it is safe now. That it is home.

Start with the floor beneath you

If you've been living in a state of chronic hypervigilance, the idea of feeling at home in your body might sound like a fantasy. It might even sound threatening. Because if you let your guard down, if you actually let yourself feel safe, what happens when the danger comes back? That's the paradox of complex trauma. Safety feels dangerous. Rest feels like a setup.

I want to talk about something that doesn't get said enough in recovery spaces. Healing can feel like loss. When you start to let go of your survival strategies - the hypervigilance, the people-pleasing, the emotional walls - it can feel like you're losing yourself. Because those strategies were you, for a very long time. They were how you knew yourself.

Gabor Mate writes about this beautifully. The true self that got buried under survival adaptations - it's still there. But meeting it can be disorienting. Who am I without the anxiety? Who am I without the need to control everything? Who am I when I'm not performing for someone else's approval? These are terrifying questions. And they don't have quick answers.

But here's what I've found. On the other side of that disorientation is something real. Something that doesn't need to perform or protect or prove. Something that can just be. It's quiet. It's simple. And it's been waiting for you this whole time. You don't have to find it all at once. You just have to be willing to keep looking. Even when it's scary. Especially when it's scary.

So you don't start with some grand practice of embodiment. You start with something smaller. You start with the floor. I mean that literally. Sit down on the floor. Feel the surface under you. Notice what it's like to be supported by something that isn't going to move or change or hurt you. Stay with me here. Just a few seconds. Notice what just happened in your body.

For me, the first time I did this, my legs started shaking. Not in a dramatic way, just a subtle tremor. I thought something was wrong. But that's actually a sign that your nervous system is starting to release some of the tension it's been holding. It's a sign that the body is beginning to trust, just a little, that it's okay to let go.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link), by Deb Dana. She's a clinician who studied under Stephen Porges, the man who developed polyvagal theory. Her approach is gentle and concrete. She teaches you how to map your nervous system, how to find your "anchors" of safety, and how to slowly expand your capacity for connection. It's not about forcing yourself to feel better. It's about giving your body the conditions it needs to settle.

You might find that when you try to be present in your body, you feel worse at first. That's normal. Your nervous system is saying, "Wait, I know this feeling, and last time I felt this, something bad happened." You have to go slow. Really slow. Like, slower than you think is possible. Because your body has its own timeline, and it doesn't care about your self-improvement goals.

Think about it. If you had been living in a war zone for twenty years, and someone came and said, "The war is over, you can relax now," you wouldn't just drop your weapon and fall asleep. You'd need to check. And check again. And check a hundred more times. That's what your nervous system is doing. It's checking to see if it's actually safe to come home.

Let the exiled parts come back

One of the most painful things about complex trauma is that you don't just lose your sense of safety. You lose parts of yourself. The parts that were too loud, or too needy, or too angry, or too sensitive. You had to lock them away to survive. And now, when you try to rebuild a home in your body, those parts start knocking. They want to come back in.

This can feel overwhelming. You might experience it as a flood of emotion, or a sudden wave of shame, or a voice that tells you you're faking it, or that you're being dramatic, or that you should just get over it already. That voice is not you. That voice is a protector part, trying to keep you safe by keeping you small. Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems (IFS), calls these parts "managers" and "firefighters." They're not bad. They're just doing a job that used to be necessary.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Schwartz's work is incredibly compassionate. He doesn't see any part of you as broken or wrong. Every part has a positive intention, even the ones that make you want to hide under the covers for three days. The goal isn't to get rid of those parts. It's to befriend them. To let them know that you're an adult now, and that you can handle things differently.

I remember working with a part of me that was terrified of being seen. It would shut down every time I tried to connect with someone. It would make my voice go flat and my eyes go blank. For years, I hated that part. I thought it was weak. I thought it was holding me back. But when I finally sat with it, I realized it was just a little kid who had learned that being seen meant being hurt. It was trying to protect me, the only way it knew how.

When I said to that part, "I see you. I understand why you're scared. You don't have to be on guard anymore. I'm here now," something shifted. Not overnight. Not in a dramatic, movie-moment way. But slowly, over weeks and months, that part started to relax. It started to trust that I could handle things. And I started to feel a little more at home in my own body.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link), by Pete Walker. He writes specifically about the experience of growing up in a traumatizing environment and the emotional flashbacks that follow. He also talks about the "inner critic," which is not a separate entity but a part of you that learned to attack you before anyone else could. His work is direct, validating, and deeply practical.

The body is not a problem to solve

At some point in this process, you might realize that you've been approaching your body as a project. Something to fix, , heal, or transcend. And that's understandable. We live in a culture that tells us we are broken and need to be repaired. But the truth is, your body is not a problem. It's a living, breathing, sentient organism that has been doing its best to keep you alive under impossible conditions.

Gabor Mate, a physician who has spent decades working with trauma and addiction, says that the question is not "What's wrong with you?" but "What happened to you?" And that shift in perspective changes everything. When you stop trying to fix yourself and start trying to understand yourself, the body starts to feel less like an enemy and more like a companion.

I know this sounds soft. I know it might feel like I'm asking you to be gentle with a body that has betrayed you, or failed you, or trapped you in pain. But that's not what I'm saying. I'm saying that the war you're fighting with your body is not your fault. It was taught to you. And it can be unlearned.

One of the most grounding practices I've found is simply placing a hand on my chest and saying, "I'm here. I'm not going anywhere." It sounds ridiculous. I felt stupid doing it at first. But it works. Because the body needs to hear, over and over, that it is not alone. That someone is home. That someone is paying attention.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link), by Lindsay C. Gibson. It helps you understand why you learned to disconnect from your body in the first place. It's not a healing manual, but it's a map. It shows you where the fault lines are, and why you had to build the defenses you did.

Rebuilding a sense of home in your own body is not a linear process. You will have days where you feel connected and present, and days where you feel like a ghost again. That's okay. That's part of the return. You're not trying to reach a permanent state of enlightenment. You're just trying to come back to yourself, again and again, with as much kindness as you can muster.

Sit with that. Notice what just happened in your body. Maybe nothing. Maybe a small shift. Maybe a tear. Whatever it is, it's okay. You're here. You're home.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - A book that many find validates their childhood experience
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate
